Anerobics Training for Competition
Bob Maplestone

Bob Maplestone, former coach at Highline CC, whose team won the 1976 and 1977 Washington CC Championships, was an All-American at Eastern Washington University. He has run a sub 4 minute mile, directed two summer cross country camps and currently coaches the Evergreen Harriers. Although this article was written for road running/racing it applies to the serious competitive orienteer as well, who wants to improves his/her times.

Most people do virtually the same training each day. They rarely change what they do from day to day or week to week throughout the year.
The individual's rate of improvement, or personal best times, in racing is slow using this method of constant training regimen. Your rate of improvement is naturally rapid when you first start running, because you are so far out of shape. Then, after one or two years of constant training, the rate is much slower and sometimes is not noticeable.

[Graph 1: BEST 10K TIMES vs MILES / WK]
most of the top USA distance runners running this fast will also be running interval training.
10KM TIMES FOR JOGGERS AND TOP RUNNERS VS MILES PER WEEK

[Graph 2: 10K TIMES vs MONTHS]
FIRST YEAR USING LONG-SLOW DISTANCE TRAINING METHOD

The above graph shows the typical rate of improvement for a beginning runner during his or her first year. Below is a second graph which shows progress for a several year period.

[Graph 3: 10K TIMES vs YEARS]
PACE TIME IMPROVEMENT FOR 10KM RACES THRU THREE YEARS

This is typical for all distances. Once you reach a certain mileage level, which is what you are prepared to run or are capable of running, it becomes difficult to improve. One way to continue improving is to continue to increase your mileage.

How can you improve your times without putting in more miles or time? Most of us are limited to running and working full time. We can't keep adding miles and time involvement. Besides, adding miles is not a sole solution because there is a point of diminishing return.

THERE IS AN ANSWER - INTERVAL AND SPEED TRAINING (ANEROBICS)
The body adapts to stress and racing is a stress. Interval and speed training of various types simulate a racing stress in a way that long slow distance (LSD) can not. Remember, though, that we still need LSD. It is still the most important part of the overall training program. But there are better ways to increase stress.

Lactic acid is produced from running faster than the body can supply oxygen to the muscles (anerobic training) and practice under these conditions improves running efficiency.

Common types of interval training are: 1) repetition running: running a set number of standard distances, each followed by a specific rest period; 2) hill running: sprinting up hills varying in length from 50 meters to 400 meters and jogging back down; and 3) Sprints: sprinting very fast for short distances from 30 meters to 200 meters and taking a long recovery between each sprint.

When should interval training be done? First, the runner needs to build a good long-slow distance base. Below is a typical program for a runner who has been running over two years. His/her mileage maximum would be less if he/she has run less than two years total training (ie, beginning runner).

Page 5

[Graph: WEEKS vs MILEAGE]
MINIMUM LONG-SLOW DISTANCE PROGRAM BEFORE INTERVALS BEGIN

Intervals should start slowly and gradually build-up just as distance did. Don't attempt to go too fast too soon. If your body is used to running slowly at 7:30/mile pace, running fast 200 meter sprints will only cause injury. A gradual build-up is what is needed here.

[Chart: INTERVAL SESSION BUILD-UP]

This is an explanation of interval session build-ups:
--Half-mile (880y) Four half-mile intervals, running each in 2:40, jogging for 3:00 between each.
--Quarter-mile (440y) Eight to ten quarter-mile intervals, running each in 75 seconds, jogging for 90 seconds between each.
--220-yard Eight to ten 220-yard intervals, running each in 35 seconds, jogging 60 seconds between each.
(The next two workouts are planned for the last four weeks of interval training)
--220-yard Three to five 220-yard sprints very fast, recovery between two and four minutes.
--110-yard Four to eight 110-yard sprints all out, walk back recovery.





These intervals can be run twice per week with a gradual increase in pace each week. Don't try to run too fast too soon. There should be a transition from LSD to sprinting. If you go too fast too soon, you will get sick or hurt.

The last weeks can be continued for a longer period if racing is your goal and you are still improving your times. Once you start to lose form, then it's time to take a week or two off and start the LSD build-up phase again.

During the first six weeks, try to keep the mileage up by jogging easy between interval days. The last four weeks or more, drop the mileage down to 30 miles/week, but keep one long run each week.

Editor's Notes

There is a quiet revolution going on in the Northwest. This region was, until comparatively recently, administered by the same overworked few and was unheard of in national and international orienteering circuits. Thinking the overworked few to be superhuman, they were allowed to drive themselves into the ground. But when they left the region, went back to other interests, or just said 'that's enough', others were forced out of their lethargy. There was a need to divide the work into smaller packages rather then try to find a few superstars to repalce those lost. We are seeing just this throughout the region.

Although a regional association is emerging, the revolution is strongest at the club level, and rightfully so. Ellensburg OC, having lost James Brat and Pat Malloy began the year rebuilding and though small in numbers they are very strong and active today. WSU-PEOC, who lost Ed O'Shaughnessy, are stilling rebuilding, while OCC and OSUOC continue to stage regular events with a small membership. Perhaps the most dramatic change is in Cascade OC. Many new event organizers have staged a varity of different events, even after losing some members that gave birth to a new club, the Nisqually Orienteers. Some current COC members are about to form another OC in the Puget Sound area. Now that's yet another way to bring more people, from within it's membership, into the administration of organizing orienteerin events.

On a larger scale, the northwest is quiently but quickly emerging on the national and international orienteering circuits as well. USOF is seeing active northwest representation and involvement in activities of the federation. Large meets across the USA and overseas are seeing participants from the northwest and their performance is taking notice.

Without a doubt, the region's emergence in the international circuits has caused the most controversy. Orienteering in the northwest has come a long way in a very short period of time compared to it's developement in the eastern part of the country. We have experienced the same growing pains, as eastern orienteering, the only difference being the length of the adjustment period. But dispite critical writings and comments, Northwest Orienteering is exploding. This premier issue is a review and preview of Northwest Orienteering and is dedicated to YOU......... the Northwest Orienteer.

British Orienteering
David Kingham

Hello, I am an orienteer from Britain and I'm living and working in the Portland area for one year. My wife, Celia, who is an occassional orienteer is here with me. Soon after arriving we met Chris Loftus, who as far as we know is the only other orienteer in the Portland area. Quite by chance we are living in the same apartment community as Chris. We intend to travel extensively in the Pacific Northwest during our time here and hopefully as well as seeing all the sights, we'll get to plenty of orienteering meets. So far we have only been to one, a night meet in Discovery Park which was most enjoyable.

Orienteering in Great Britain is different in many ways from orienteering in the USA. Perhaps the greatest difference is the very much higher density of orienteers in G.B., there are also very few forest, particularly in the South of England, so that almost all the available area have been mapped and used for recent meets.

My home club is Cambridge University OC which has been one of the strongest clubs in Britain in recent years, despite the nearest wood or forest of any sort being 40 miles aways. The land around Cambridge is very flat, we have to drive at least 150 miles to find a decent size hill. The nicest orienteering area near Cambridge is Sandingham Country Park which we have used for the Midlands Orienteering Championships. Sandingham House belongs to the Queen and it was there that romance blossomed between Prince Charles and Lady Diana. While we were checking the map and planning the courses for the Midlans Championships, the whole area was full of press photographers and journalists, but unfortunately they were not too interested in reporting the orienteering. By the day of the meet, Charles and Diana had gone elsewhere so the place was alot quieter, until the 1500 competitors arrived, that is!

Each Region of Britain holds its own orienteering championships, these regions are Scotland, Wales, Nathan, Ireland and Northern, Southern and Midlands in England. These events, plus the British Championship and the Jan Kjellstrom Trophy weekend are the premier meets in Britain each year. The standard of mapping, course planning and meet organization is usually very high and typically one to two thousand people from all over Britain go to each of these meets. Our other national meets are called Badge Events and they usually attract about 500 people. If someone consistently finishes within the winners time plus 100% on their course they are awarded a bronze badge if they finish within winners time plus 50% they get a silver badge and for winners time plus 25% a gold badge.

Most of our meets however are just small local meets, sometimes with color coded courses or run in conjunction with a school's league. Usually the maps for these meets are four or five color, but we still use black and white maps on occassions. I remember one meet where they had a black and brown map, but unfortunately the register had shifted during printing so that the brown was nearly half an inch wrong compared to the black. This was bad enough, but they ran out of maps and the last atarters had to use photocopies of these shifted maps. Apparently that made things very difficult! I wish you all better luck with your map making.
Good Orienteering!

Washington Interscholastic O-League

The first two meets have been held in a six-meet series of interscholastic orienteering competitions that will culminate in a championship meet February 26 at St. Edward's State Park. The league is being organized by the Cascade Orienteering Club, with school competition held as part of regularly scheduled club meets in the season between cross-country and track competition in the schools.

Any school-age competitor who has not yet graduated from high school is eligible for the meets, whether or not his or her school can field a team. The team competition is being scored on the same basis as cross-country meets, with the sum of the first five placings of each team giving team score (low total wins). There is no limit on team size, but only the first seven placers for a team will count in the standings from which the team scores are derived. In other words, there is a premium on team depth, since 6th and 7th finishers can push down members of other teams, even though 6th and 7th placings will not be added as part of the team score.

Teams currently are co-ed, and there is no division of high school from junior high competitors. When enough teams have joined the league, some further division for the calculation of team scores may be possible. In the championship meet, there will be a team trophy and separate awards for both boys and girls in high school and junior high categories.

Individuals and teams may join the league at any time during the season. The fee for the full series is $10 per person (this includes registration fees and pays for the trophies), but that will be prorated for those who participate in fewer meets. The remaining meets are: January 8 --Camp Long; Jan. 22--Seward Park; Feb. 5--Carkeek Park; Feb. 26--St. Edwards. Starts in each meet, 10AM-12.

In the meets to date, four schools have participated: Nathan Hale, Lakeside, Collins, and Roosevelt. Nathan Hale's team includes many of its strong cross-country team that finished a close second in the Seattle Metro League boy's championship. Among those who have orienteered are Brent Felt (first in Metro and second in the state championship for AAA schools) and Geoff Dillard (fourth in Metro). It is anticipated that members of the Hale Metro Champion girl's CC team will be participating in the remaining O meets. Lakeside is another Seattle cross-country power, although much of its orienteering team has come from students involved in the active wilderness program at Lakeside rather than from CC.
In the remaining meets, we hope...

JUNIORS
Summer Development Trip to Europe

In traveling to Europe and Canada these past two summers it has become apparent to us that our Juniors would benefit from a chance to do more orienteering against more competitive fields. Most European countries and Canada have well developed programs which attract and retain the interest of junior age orienteers. In this country many juniors orienteer too infrequently or win too easily to maintain an interest in orienteering.

With this in mind, Linda and I would like to offer a chance for juniors, ages 13-18, to join us on a trip to Europe this summer. We have in mind a low budget trip that will take in the Norwegian Sørlandsgallopen (6 days), the Swedish O-ringen (5 days) and the Scottish Highland Games (6 Days). We have numerous contacts in all these countries and are fairly certain that we will be able to arrange some visits with orienteering families. In addition to these scheduled meets we will try to arrange as many training sessions as possible with local clubs along the way.

We have both been teaching for over ten years and have a great deal of experience leading groups of students in this age group on numerous outdoor type outings. In addition we have a great deal of travel experience including a whole summer of orienteering in Europe two summers ago. We know how to travel cheaply through many years of practice.

The details of the trip will be worked out when we become aware of sufficient interest, but it should go something like this: Depart from an East coast city during the first week in July, spend five weeks in Europe, and return the second week in August. The emphasis will be on orienteering, but we will take a few side trips for hiking, swimming and sightseeing, etc. We will camp, stay in hostels and with clubs and families. We hope that the cost will be about $1000. This will include everything except the cost for getting to and from the east coast. We will travel by boat, trains and buses.

Our trip might be able to tie in with some of the adults' plans to attend the Veterans cup in Finland. This takes place at the same time as the Sørlandsgallopen in Norway. Kids could go with us and we could all meet up again at the O-ringen a week later. We are very flexible and are open to any suggestions.

A trip like this requires a great deal of advance planning. Entries for the the O-ringen need to be in almost four months in advance. If you are interested we need to know of your interest as soon as possible. Please write or call us before January 15, 1983.
Rick Worner & Linda Kohn
105 Eglantine Road
Rochester, NY 14616
(716) 621-5655 (evenings)
We are very excited about doing this trip and think that those of you who join us will find it a rewarding and fun experience.

Orienteering in Australia
Karl Nolte

Karl and Elaine Nolte are COC members who moved to Perth, Australia this past summer. Karl was very active in the early years of the club and was the president in 1978.

Well after being here for three months and watching the papers every weekend to see the results of the weekly orienteering meets, I finally got out to the 'bush' and took part. The particular meet I went to was attended by over 200 people and there were plenty of picnics spread out after the run. This is a common occurance 'down here'. Australia is very family and sport oriented and it is very common for a lot of people to show up for the meets. The results of the courses (5 the day I went) are in the paper on Monday. They even have a trailer that is used as the HQ area. It is really big time, but like in Sweden and Canada it is backed by the government and it gets a few bucks to stay alive. The particular course that I took the family on was run as a benefit for the handicaped kids center.

There are a lot of permanent courses in the National Parks that can be used at any time like the one in Stanley Park in Vancouver, B.C. You will notice on the maps that the contour lines are marked but there is no elevation given. I don't know why at this point it is that way, but I figure it is because that most of this country is flat or at least lacks any great height. This course was in the Darling Range which is the mountain range outside of Perth. I think the highest point is less than 2000 feet in elevation. That is some height for the country around here. The course was near the tops of those ranges and there were a few areas of quite steep climbs. Other than the topography the area is great for the sport. The vegetation is fairly low growing 'bush' with a lot of trails and tracks. There are trees here but they are few and far between and other than the occasional pine forest, most of the trees are gum or eucalyptus.

Currently it is Spring and the wildflowers are in bloom and it was a most beautiful course to just relax and walk. That is exactly what we did. The course had 15 controls points, but as you can see from the map they were all pretty much in the open. I hope Elaine warms up to the sport as land navigation is a MUST in the outback and when you are used to the sun in the Southern sky and here it is in the Northern sky it is really easy to get 'turned around' when you are tired. Of course we have the Southern Cross to guide us home but you must wait for dark to get that help. Compass needles still point North here but I seem to feel that they drift a bit more. Maybe that is just a feeling I have after not running with one for a few months. Silva is used alot here, but but we do have access to other brands as well. As for clothing...I did not see any shorts until the end of the meet at the picnic area and it was in the high 60's. I think it is because of the brush and the bugs, etc. that long pants are worn during the meet. I have yet to experience the heat of the Summer and I don't know how the sport keeps it up in 90 degree heat but in a couple of months I'll know more. I can't see running a marathon in this weather either but they do it.

Anyway I hope all is well in the Cascades. I will be digging out my COC T-shirt for the next meet to put on a little pose. Best to all of you in God's Country...the Pacific Northwest!

USOF & IOF NEWS

Sweden had 150,000 people trying TRIM Orienteering in 1982....65% of these were previously non-orienteers.

Hong Kong OA is joining the IOF, becoming the 29th member of the world body. There are now 6 member countries in Asia: India, Japan, Korea, Singapore, Israel and Hong Kong. Hong Kong has 11 orienteering clubs with a combined membership in excess of 4000.

Future World Championships

Ski-WOC 1986 in Bulgaria
WOC 1987 in France

Orienteering will be a demonstration event at the 1988 Summer Olympics in Seoul, Korea and at the 1988 Winter Olympics in Calgary, Canada.

Bruce Wolfe, BAOC, placed seventh (59.44) behind the winner from Finland (51.57) in an international event in Spa, Belgium this past fall.

Orienteering in the Olympics? This question was discussed at the IOF congress in Belgium in August and at the Olympic congress in Baden-Baden in September, some decisions were made that are negative for the olympic fans. Among other things it was established that those sport branches which wish to participate in the olympic summer games must be practised regulary in at least 50 countries on three continents. The IOF has at the moment 29 member countries, so many years will probably pass until this is real. The ski-orienteering has the greatest chances to be approved first because winter sports only require a minimum of 25 countries. For some reasons, the demand is even lower for the number of countries regarding female sports (only 35 countries for summer games). Perhaps female orienteers will participate in the olympic games long befor the male orienteers!

If you are interested in working on the USOF Clubs Committee contact it's chairmen: Clayton Smith, 4338 East F Street, Tacoma, WA 98404 (206)474-5675.

Challenge Cup

How about some friendly inter-club competition at regional orienteering meets? I am offering to orienteering clubs in the Northwest the following challenge: a travelling award to be given to the club with the best performance at a Class A or B meet based on the following criteria:

  1. The event must be either Class A or Class B or the Canadian equivalent.
  2. The event must be held in the states of Washington, Oregon, Idaho, and Montana (west of 110% west longitude) or the provinces of British Columbia and Alberta.
  3. Notice of the event must have been published at least three months in advance, specifying that it is a challenge cup event (for multi-day events only one day is involved in the challenge and the notice must indicate which day this will be); also, the notice must describe the award and these rules (at least the point system described below).
  4. The winning club is the club that accumulates the most points based on the following system:
    a) The winner of the most difficult men's course earns 20 points.
    b) For each minute (or fraction) that they are behind the winner, other finishers earn one less point (example: winner - 68:00, next - 67:45, third - 65:59, gives winner 20 points, next 19 points, third 17 points) note that only the course winner earns the full 20 points except in the case of an exact tie.
    c) Only the top three from each club count towards the club total.
    d) Women accumulate points form their most difficult course in a similar manner with only the best three for from each club contributing towards the club total.
    e) A club also earns a point for each club member that finishes at least one of the courses offered at the event (no double counting for participants who elect to run several courses the same day)
    f) In the event of a tie between clubs, the award shall be presented to both clubs jointly.
  5. Only those clubs charted by USOF or COF licated in the region described in section 2 are eligible for this challenge.
    6a) Only those individuals who have been members of their club for more than three months and are citizens of either the USA or Canada may have their results included in the computations listed in sections 4a-4d, (for the purposes of sections 4a-4d 'winner' is that person with the best time who meets the requirements of this section).
    b) Any member of a club may be counted under section 4e provided that they are counted by only one club.
    7a) The winning club is obligated to host a similar 'challenge' event within nine months.
    b) If several clubs jointly hold the award, they shall determine which club shall host the next event, ultimately by a coin toss.
  6. If the winning club does not announce a 'challenge' event within six months after receiving the award, the award reverts to previous winner who in turn is obligated to host a 'challenge' event.
  7. If no 'challenge' event is announced within twelve months the award reverts to the sponsor (me).
  8. I will also exercise the sponsor's prerogative in claiming the right to change these rules as I see fit or to repossess the award at any time.

Let's give this a try and see how it works. We may need to adjust the point system but let's see what happens for the first few meets. I know this will violate my own rules, but I'd like to make Cascade OC's St. Edwards State Park meet on February 26 as the first 'challenge' event. FORUM

Meet classification systems are important for two reasons: 1) to help orienteers know what to expect at any specific event in terms of map quality, categories of competition, cost, awards, "scope" of the event (regional, local, etc.), and other factors; and 2) to assist orienteers in selecting between meets to attend based on these factors. A simple and unambiguous classification system is clearly valuable as it allows us to efficiently define the quality and characteristics of a meet.
The accepted system in the United States is that advocated by USOF. In this system, meets are labeled class-A, -B, or -C. Class-A sanctioning, the top rating, requires not only considerable investment to produce the high quality maps that all clubs should strive for, but sanctioning also requires time so that the USOF sanctioning committee can approve the application. With these tight controls, current class-A usually cost more and nearly always offer excellent competition as well as banquets and other social extras. And, they are the only meets at which an individual can earn national ranking points.
Class-B and -C meets, on the other hand, are not really nationally sanctioned as no approval is necessary from USOF. For clubs that host class-A events, these other labels usually imply relatively inferior meets. Other clubs, however, may host non-class-A events that in terms of orienteering are just as good as a class-A event. For example, a club may be affiliated with USOF but not wish to spend the time pursuing national sanctioning; or a club may not be USOF-affiliated and is thus not eligible to receive snactioning. Other clubs may have excellent terrain and may be capable of setting top-notch courses, but due to a lack of funds cannot afford a multicolor map; consequently, no matter how good their meet is orienteering-wise it cannot be sanctioned class-A. Clubs such as these may host meets that are first-class, yet the current classification system fails to label them as class-A. And, while those close to USOF will tend to rate these meets as inferior to class-A meets, others not so affiliated will rate them top notch.
Here in the Pacific Northwest this classification system is further complicated by the existance of a similar yet significantly different system in Canada.
Clearly, the current system is ambiguous. An improved system might serve us better. Having given this problem much thought, the new system that I found most effective is:

MEET TYPES:

  1. Introductory- formal event designed specifically for first-time orienteers (no-vices); e.g., workshops in small parks sponsored by a Parks Dept. No courses for experienced orienteers (non-first-timers).
  2. Local- formal event offering courses both for novices and as a practice for local experienced orienteers. Generally three courses.
  3. Regional- a meet designed for regional competition. Generally 5+ courses; the equity of competition is essential. Awards given.
  4. National- same as a regional event but including the award of a national title.
  5. Practice- informal event for experienced orienteers.

MEET QUALITY STANDARD:
Larger events (regional and national) provide equitable competition and thus may be used for a national or regional ranking system. Any competitor who feels that a course fails to meet the basic standard of "equitable competition" may protest to have the results voided. A protest committee at the meet will decide if the protest is valid.

SANCTIONED VS. UNSANCTIONED:
Sanctioned meets may be labeled as above, plus the fact that they are "USOF-sanctioned" may be added.
Any comments or suggestions for the improvement of this system may be directed to Jeff Lewis, c/o OSUOC.

SCHOOLS

Teaching Orienteering to Children
Lorrie Bouchard
Lorrie Bouchard is a fifth grade teacher at Manchester School in the Parkway School District near St. Louis, Missouri. This article first appeared in OUSA, Summer 1978.

Rumors of field trips and getting lost in the woods buzz through my fifth grade class. They enjoy the prospect of the challenge of getting outdoors, and probably most of all, it doesn't look like math.
I know of no more appealing topic which introduces and applies the multitude of concepts and skills inherent to the sport of orienteering: map reading, compass use, route selection, calculating time, ecology, sportsmanship and a host of other topics that fringe on every discipline taught in elementary school. From lessons in geography to art to creative writing, orienteering is valuable.
Orienteering does not fit squarely into one of the standard school disciplines which makes it difficult to introduce in view of the current "back-to-basics" atmosphere today. To some people orienteering looks like too much fun to be learning. However, orienteering is accountable in a "basics" program because it actively applies useful basic skills to real problems. Application of orienteering skills combined with mild competition helps to enhance and motivate learning.
A novice need not feel afraid to teach the sport; after all, orienteering is still brand-new. If you are willing to jump in and develop your own skills by trying the training activities yourself, you will find your confidence grows with the excitement of the sport. There are teaching materials available to help. Many of the orienteering books now on the market are excellent.
Skills come more slowly to younger children. A one-shot, four-hour workshop culminating in a mini-orienteering event can be too much for younger students to digest at once. A month or two of work developing a spiral of skills is more feasible. Reduce the possibility of overwhelming the children by not requiring them to apply too many new skills too quickly. At the same time, do not let the children lose interest.
Map skills come first. The map is the most important tool in orienteering but it also frightens young students. It looks very complicated and confusing and is not as gimmicky as the compass. The compass is the secondary tool in orienteering and unless it is taught last, the students will not be motivated to understand the map.
Explore a variety of maps with the children to determine how an orienteering map differs from road maps, schoolground maps or world maps. Discuss the legends of the different maps and how the map symbols on the orienteering maps are useful to the orienteer. Common symbols should be memorized and can be reinforced by a commercially available relay game.
The contour lines are generally the most confusing map detail, but I find having the children draw their own contour lines illuminates the concept. Build several didderent topographic models illustrating land forms such as a re-entrant, a cliff, a saddle and a knoll. Place these in containers deep enough to add at least two or three inches of water. Clear containers are best, but baking pans do well. Have students add water at half-inch intervals and sketch each successive water line as viewed from above.
Cardinal and intercardinal directions should be memorized. Following these directions can be strengthened by having the students make line designs on graph paper. The students must then indicate a starting point on the design and write compass directions for following the design. Students trade designs to test the accuracy of directions.
The students can now learn to orient their maps by keeping certain major features oriented in their actual relationship to the mapholder. Take a walk with the student and check that each

Page 20

student keeps his map oriented through changes in direction. Now is the appropriate time to teach the technique of using the thumb to follow one's location on the map.
Scale is less confusing to the student if you stick to either the english or metric system. Use the map scale to measure distances on different maps. Calculating exact distances can be difficult for younger children so teach them to estimate whenever practical by rounding off distances or by using parts of the hand in place of a ruler when calculating distances on the map.
Set up a 100 meter course and have each student calculate his average walking and jogging paces. Go on to mathematically calculate fractional and multipule distances, e.g. 50 meters, 300 meters. Calculating the number of paces to travel 350 meters based on a paces per hundred meter average is a simple task for an adult, but for children it is quite complex as it requires a logical application of several different arithmetic tasks. Go slowly here and give the students plenty of practice exercises until they learn to apply the abstractions of multiplication, addition, and division at the correct times.
Route choice is yet another skill that can be taught before the compass. Students should be encouraged to estimate distances to be travelled as they discuss route options.
As the compass is introduced, give the children ample practice at dialing in given bearings and facing those directions. Go on to give them several opportunities to actually travel a particular distance. The competitive compass game published by Silva is fun and there are several other good games/exercises described in James Gilchrist's book, Teaching Orienteering.
Stress to the children to rely on the map. Compass bearings only need to be taken when orienting the map, when double checking their route, when they are not certain whether to go right, go left, go backwards, can recognize no major features or to use a panic azimuth.
Plan the orienteering to give all students a feeling of success. Students will feel safer if they travel in pairs and are fully aware of safety measures. Award prizes or some sort of recognition to all who complete the course.
Each child who experiences success in orienteering helps to assure the future of "The Thinking Sport" in America and learns math!

An Orienteering Game for 3rd Graders
Beth Skelton

My situation was this: I had about 45 minutes with twenty Brownies to do some kind of Orienteering activity. I had a lovely little schoolyard map and plenty of enthusiastic adult help. So with some lead-up, I decided to set them loose on a "Score Orienteering" course.

Inside in the Brownie Ring, we talked about all different kinds of maps. The girls explained to me that "with a map you can go someplace you've never been before" --- whether it's a new friends house or a vaction spot in Oklahoma. We decided to find ourselves, right at that very minute. We found the U.S. on the map, then Missouri, then Webster Groves. We were getting close. Then I held up the Anninciation School map. "What's this?" Nobody knew. They had never seen anything quite like it. When somebody said it was their school they all made a grab for it. What commotion! They sat still long enough to go through the legend and get a feel for the map --- then it was a race out to the starting point, the flag pole.

Before the meeting I had taped up 3" X 3" markers made of red and white construction paper to the various trees, doors, fence posts, etc. I marked these on the maps with red circles.

Each pair of girls had a map and a pencil. I told them if they found all the markers and wrote the letters down in the right order, it would spell a word. From the start, they flew

off in all directions to find the "controls" and put the code letters on their map. Some followed the traditional method of looking on their maps to see where, say #4 was, then going there. Others spotted the markers first then figured out which one they were at. Later we all gathered breathlessly back at the flagpole. Everyone had a good time, even the leaders. Together we looked at our maps and spelled out OR-IE-NT-EE-RI-NG --- a new word for a fun game.

All events sponsored by the Nisqually Orienteers are being organized under a new system that divides up the organizational responsibility and work. Although there is no limit to the number of people who can share the tasks of an event, each event will have three key individuals; an event organizer, a course planner, and a controller. Once a date and place for an event is scheduled, the three key individuals will be designated as soon as possible. The
The responsibilities of the event organizer is mostly administrative, such as placing out directional signs to the registration area, taking care of registration and the start/finish area(s). The course planner and controller will work closely together, but each has their own responsibility and it helps of they have been an event organizer or assistant. Basically the course planner designs and sets the courses and after the event insures the control markers are retreived. Prior to the event, the controller reviews the courses to insure they are designed for the appropriate skill level inaddition to being a general advisor to the course planner and event organizer. On the day of the event, the controller is the vetter (insures the control markers are properly marked and located, checks the control description sheets and master maps for accuracy).
During the past nine months, 13 events have been sponsored by the club and although there has been many people who have helped with the administration of these events, the same can not be said about the courses and that's what we enjoy so much. The courses for those 13 events were done by three individuals (11 of the 13 by the same person) and a scout troop did two novice courses for one event. One of the three individuals had never designed & set courses before, for that event and the two courses set by the scouts, a more experienced person worked with them. The many people who have assisted past organizers are encouraged to volunteer as event organizers or course planners and people who have never helped out before are encouraged to be event organizers or assist them at least once this year. In most cases, organizers and assistants can complete one of the courses for free in exchange for their efforts.

Anerobics Training for Competition

Bob Maplestone, former coach at Highline CC, whose team won the 1976 and 1977 Washington CC Championships, was an All-American at Eastern Washington University. He has run a sub 4 minute mile, directed two summer cross country camps and currently coaches the Evergreen Harriers. Although this article was written for road running/racing it applies to the serious competitive orienteer as well, who wants to improves his/her times.
Most people do virtually the same training each day. They rarely change what they do from day to day or week to week throughout the year.
[Graph 1: BEST 10K TIMES vs MILES / WK]
[Graph 2: 10K TIMES vs MONTHS]
The above graph shows the typical rate of improvement for a beginning runner during his or her first year. Below is a second graph which shows progress for a several year period.
[Graph 3: 10K TIMES vs YEARS]

This is typical for all distances. Once you reach a certain mileage level, which is what you are prepared to run or are capable of running, it becomes difficult to improve. One way to continue improving is to continue to increase your mileage.

How can you improve your times without putting in more miles or time? Most of us are limited to running and working full time. We can't keep adding miles and time involvement. Besides, adding miles is not a sole solution because there is a point of diminishing return.
THERE IS AN ANSWER - INTERVAL AND SPEED TRAINING (ANEROBICS)
Lactic acid is produced from running faster than the body can supply oxygen to the muscles (anerobic training) and practice under these conditions improves running efficiency.

Common types of interval training are: 1) repetition running: running a set number of standard distances, each followed by a specific rest period; 2) hill running: sprinting up hills varying in length from 50 meters to 400 meters and jogging back down; and 3) Sprints: sprinting very fast for short distances from 30 meters to 200 meters and taking a long recovery between each sprint.

When should interval training be done? First, the runner needs to build a good long-slow distance base. Below is a typical program for a runner who has been running over two years. His/her mileage maximum would be less if he/she has run less than two years total training (ie, beginning runner).
Page 5
[Graph: WEEKS vs MILEAGE]

Intervals should start slowly and gradually build-up just as distance did. Don't attempt to go too fast too soon. If your body is used to running slowly at 7:30/mile pace, running fast 200 meter sprints will only cause injury. A gradual build-up is what is needed here.
[Chart: INTERVAL SESSION BUILD-UP]
This is an explanation of interval session build-ups:

These intervals can be run twice per week with a gradual increase in pace each week. Don't try to run too fast too soon. There should be a transition from LSD to sprinting. If you go too fast too soon, you will get sick or hurt.

The last weeks can be continued for a longer period if racing is your goal and you are still improving your times. Once you start to lose form, then it's time to take a week or two off and start the LSD build-up phase again.
During the first six weeks, try to keep the mileage up by jogging easy between interval days. The last four weeks or more, drop the mileage down to 30 miles/week, but keep one long run each week.
Editor's Notes

There is a quiet revolution going on in the Northwest. This region was, until comparatively recently, administered by the same overworked few and was unheard of in national and international orienteering circuits. Thinking the overworked few to be superhuman, they were allowed to drive themselves into the ground. But when they left the region, went back to other interests, or just said 'that's enough', others were forced out of their lethargy. There was a need to divide the work into smaller packages rather then try to find a few superstars to repalce those lost. We are seeing just this throughout the region.

Although a regional association is emerging, the revolution is strongest at the club level, and rightfully so. Ellensburg OC, having lost James Brat and Pat Malloy began the year rebuilding and though small in numbers they are very strong and active today. WSU-PEOC, who lost Ed O'Shaughnessy, are stilling rebuilding, while OCC and OSUOC continue to stage regular events with a small membership. Perhaps the most dramatic change is in Cascade OC. Many new event organizers have staged a varity of different events, even after losing some members that gave birth to a new club, the Nisqually Orienteers. Some current COC members are about to form another OC in the Puget Sound area. Now that's yet another way to bring more people, from within it's membership, into the administration of organizing orienteerin events.

On a larger scale, the northwest is quiently but quickly emerging on the national and international orienteering circuits as well. USOF is seeing active northwest representation and involvement in activities of the federation. Large meets across the USA and overseas are seeing participants from the northwest and their performance is taking notice.

Without a doubt, the region's emergence in the international circuits has caused the most controversy. Orienteering in the northwest has come a long way in a very short period of time compared to it's developement in the eastern part of the country. We have experienced the same growing pains, as eastern orienteering, the only difference being the length of the adjustment period. But dispite critical writings and comments, Northwest Orienteering is exploding. This premier issue is a review and preview of Northwest Orienteering and is dedicated to YOU......... the Northwest Orienteer.

British Orienteering

Hello, I am an orienteer from Britain and I'm living and working in the Portland area for one year. My wife, Celia, who is an occassional orienteer is here with me. Soon after arriving we met Chris Loftus, who as far as we know is the only other orienteer in the Portland area. Quite by chance we are living in the same apartment community as Chris. We intend to travel extensively in the Pacific Northwest during our time here and hopefully as well as seeing all the sights, we'll get to plenty of orienteering meets. So far we have only been to one, a night meet in Discovery Park which was most enjoyable.

Orienteering in Great Britain is different in many ways from orienteering in the USA. Perhaps the greatest difference is the very much higher density of orienteers in G.B., there are also very few forest, particularly in the South of England, so that almost all the available area have been mapped and used for recent meets.

My home club is Cambridge University OC which has been one of the strongest clubs in Britain in recent years, despite the nearest wood or forest of any sort being 40 miles aways. The land around Cambridge is very flat, we have to drive at least 150 miles to find a decent size hill. The nicest orienteering area near Cambridge is Sandingham Country Park which we have used for the Midlands Orienteering Championships. Sandingham House belongs to the Queen and it was there that romance blossomed between Prince Charles and Lady Diana. While we were checking the map and planning the courses for the Midlans Championships, the whole area was full of press photographers and journalists, but unfortunately they were not too interested in reporting the orienteering. By the day of the meet, Charles and Diana had gone elsewhere so the place was alot quieter, until the 1500 competitors arrived, that is!

Each Region of Britain holds its own orienteering championships, these regions are Scotland, Wales, Nathan, Ireland and Northern, Southern and Midlands in England. These events, plus the British Championship and the Jan Kjellstrom Trophy weekend are the premier meets in Britain each year. The standard of mapping, course planning and meet organization is usually very high and typically one to two thousand people from all over Britain go to each of these meets. Our other national meets are called Badge Events and they usually attract about 500 people. If someone consistently finishes within the winners time plus 100% on their course they are awarded a bronze badge if they finish within winners time plus 50% they get a silver badge and for winners time plus 25% a gold badge.

Most of our meets however are just small local meets, sometimes with color coded courses or run in conjunction with a school's league. Usually the maps for these meets are four or five color, but we still use black and white maps on occassions. I remember one meet where they had a black and brown map, but unfortunately the register had shifted during printing so that the brown was nearly half an inch wrong compared to the black. This was bad enough, but they ran out of maps and the last atarters had to use photocopies of these shifted maps. Apparently that made things very difficult! I wish you all better luck with your map making.

Washington Interscholastic O-League

The first two meets have been held in a six-meet series of interscholastic orienteering competitions that will culminate in a championship meet February 26 at St. Edward's State Park. The league is being organized by the Cascade Orienteering Club, with school competition held as part of regularly scheduled club meets in the season between cross-country and track competition in the schools.

Any school-age competitor who has not yet graduated from high school is eligible for the meets, whether or not his or her school can field a team. The team competition is being scored on the same basis as cross-country meets, with the sum of the first five placings of each team giving team score (low total wins). There is no limit on team size, but only the first seven placers for a team will count in the standings from which the team scores are derived. In other words, there is a premium on team depth, since 6th and 7th finishers can push down members of other teams, even though 6th and 7th placings will not be added as part of the team score.

Teams currently are co-ed, and there is no division of high school from junior high competitors. When enough teams have joined the league, some further division for the calculation of team scores may be possible. In the championship meet, there will be a team trophy and separate awards for both boys and girls in high school and junior high categories.

Individuals and teams may join the league at any time during the season. The fee for the full series is $10 per person (this includes registration fees and pays for the trophies), but that will be prorated for those who participate in fewer meets. The remaining meets are: January 8 --Camp Long; Jan. 22--Seward Park; Feb. 5--Carkeek Park; Feb. 26--St. Edwards. Starts in each meet, 10AM-12.

In the meets to date, four schools have participated: Nathan Hale, Lakeside, Collins, and Roosevelt. Nathan Hale's team includes many of its strong cross-country team that finished a close second in the Seattle Metro League boy's championship. Among those who have orienteered are Brent Felt (first in Metro and second in the state championship for AAA schools) and Geoff Dillard (fourth in Metro). It is anticipated that members of the Hale Metro Champion girl's CC team will be participating in the remaining O meets. Lakeside is another Seattle cross-country power, although much of its orienteering team has come from students involved in the active wilderness program at Lakeside rather than from CC. In the remaining meets, we hope...

JUNIORS Summer Development Trip to Europe

In traveling to Europe and Canada these past two summers it has become apparent to us that our Juniors would benefit from a chance to do more orienteering against more competitive fields. Most European countries and Canada have well developed programs which attract and retain the interest of junior age orienteers. In this country many juniors orienteer too infrequently or win too easily to maintain an interest in orienteering.

With this in mind, Linda and I would like to offer a chance for juniors, ages 13-18, to join us on a trip to Europe this summer. We have in mind a low budget trip that will take in the Norwegian Sørlandsgallopen (6 days), the Swedish O-ringen (5 days) and the Scottish Highland Games (6 Days). We have numerous contacts in all these countries and are fairly certain that we will be able to arrange some visits with orienteering families. In addition to these scheduled meets we will try to arrange as many training sessions as possible with local clubs along the way.

We have both been teaching for over ten years and have a great deal of experience leading groups of students in this age group on numerous outdoor type outings. In addition we have a great deal of travel experience including a whole summer of orienteering in Europe two summers ago. We know how to travel cheaply through many years of practice.

The details of the trip will be worked out when we become aware of sufficient interest, but it should go something like this: Depart from an East coast city during the first week in July, spend five weeks in Europe, and return the second week in August. The emphasis will be on orienteering, but we will take a few side trips for hiking, swimming and sightseeing, etc. We will camp, stay in hostels and with clubs and families. We hope that the cost will be about $1000. This will include everything except the cost for getting to and from the east coast. We will travel by boat, trains and buses.

Our trip might be able to tie in with some of the adults' plans to attend the Veterans cup in Finland. This takes place at the same time as the Sørlandsgallopen in Norway. Kids could go with us and we could all meet up again at the O-ringen a week later. We are very flexible and are open to any suggestions.

A trip like this requires a great deal of advance planning. Entries for the the O-ringen need to be in almost four months in advance. If you are interested we need to know of your interest as soon as possible. Please write or call us before January 15, 1983. Orienteering in Australia Karl Nolte
Karl and Elaine Nolte are COC members who moved to Perth, Australia this past summer. Karl was very active in the early years of the club and was the president in 1978.

Well after being here for three months and watching the papers every weekend to see the results of the weekly orienteering meets, I finally got out to the 'bush' and took part. The particular meet I went to was attended by over 200 people and there were plenty of picnics spread out after the run. This is a common occurance 'down here'. Australia is very family and sport oriented and it is very common for a lot of people to show up for the meets. The results of the courses (5 the day I went) are in the paper on Monday. They even have a trailer that is used as the HQ area. It is really big time, but like in Sweden and Canada it is backed by the government and it gets a few bucks to stay alive. The particular course that I took the family on was run as a benefit for the handicaped kids center.

There are a lot of permanent courses in the National Parks that can be used at any time like the one in Stanley Park in Vancouver, B.C. You will notice on the maps that the contour lines are marked but there is no elevation given. I don't know why at this point it is that way, but I figure it is because that most of this country is flat or at least lacks any great height. This course was in the Darling Range which is the mountain range outside of Perth. I think the highest point is less than 2000 feet in elevation. That is some height for the country around here. The course was near the tops of those ranges and there were a few areas of quite steep climbs. Other than the topography the area is great for the sport. The vegetation is fairly low growing 'bush' with a lot of trails and tracks. There are trees here but they are few and far between and other than the occasional pine forest, most of the trees are gum or eucalyptus.

Currently it is Spring and the wildflowers are in bloom and it was a most beautiful course to just relax and walk. That is exactly what we did. The course had 15 controls points, but as you can see from the map they were all pretty much in the open. I hope Elaine warms up to the sport as land navigation is a MUST in the outback and when you are used to the sun in the Southern sky and here it is in the Northern sky it is really easy to get 'turned around' when you are tired. Of course we have the Southern Cross to guide us home but you must wait for dark to get that help. Compass needles still point North here but I seem to feel that they drift a bit more. Maybe that is just a feeling I have after not running with one for a few months. Silva is used alot here, but but we do have access to other brands as well. As for clothing...I did not see any shorts until the end of the meet at the picnic area and it was in the high 60's. I think it is because of the brush and the bugs, etc. that long pants are worn during the meet. I have yet to experience the heat of the Summer and I don't know how the sport keeps it up in 90 degree heat but in a couple of months I'll know more. I can't see running a marathon in this weather either but they do it.
Anyway I hope all is well in the Cascades. I will be digging out my COC T-shirt for the next meet to put on a little pose. Best to all of you in God's Country...the Pacific Northwest!

How about some friendly inter-club competition at regional orienteering meets? I am offering to orienteering clubs in the Northwest the following challenge: a travelling award to be given to the club with the best performance at a Class A or B meet based on the following criteria:
The event must be either Class A or Class B or the Canadian equivalent.
The event must be held in the states of Washington, Oregon, Idaho, and Montana (west of 110% west longitude) or the provinces of British Columbia and Alberta.

Notice of the event must have been published at least three months in advance, specifying that it is a challenge cup event (for multi-day events only one day is involved in the challenge and the notice must indicate which day this will be); also, the notice must describe the award and these rules (at least the point system described below).

The winning club is the club that accumulates the most points based on the following system: a) The winner of the most difficult men's course earns 20 points. b) For each minute (or fraction) that they are behind the winner, other finishers earn one less point (example: winner - 68:00, next - 67:45, third - 65:59, gives winner 20 points, next 19 points, third 17 points) note that only the course winner earns the full 20 points except in the case of an exact tie. c) Only the top three from each club count towards the club total. d) Women accumulate points form their most difficult course in a similar manner with only the best three for from each club contributing towards the club total. e) A club also earns a point for each club member that finishes at least one of the courses offered at the event (no double counting for participants who elect to run several courses the same day) f) In the event of a tie between clubs, the award shall be presented to both clubs jointly.

Only those clubs charted by USOF or COF licated in the region described in section 2 are eligible for this challenge. 6a) Only those individuals who have been members of their club for more than three months and are citizens of either the USA or Canada may have their results included in the computations listed in sections 4a-4d, (for the purposes of sections 4a-4d 'winner' is that person with the best time who meets the requirements of this section). b) Any member of a club may be counted under section 4e provided that they are counted by only one club. 7a) The winning club is obligated to host a similar 'challenge' event within nine months. b) If several clubs jointly hold the award, they shall determine which club shall host the next event, ultimately by a coin toss.
If the winning club does not announce a 'challenge' event within six months after receiving the award, the award reverts to previous winner who in turn is obligated to host a 'challenge' event.
If no 'challenge' event is announced within twelve months the award reverts to the sponsor (me).
I will also exercise the sponsor's prerogative in claiming the right to change these rules as I see fit or to repossess the award at any time.

COC'S LONG-RANGE PLANNING In several meetings in the autumn of 1982, COC drew up plans for several major meets in the coming three years. Details remain to be worked out, and changes are possible. Any comments or suggestions should be sent to COC in plenty of time for adjustments to be made, should conflicts or other problems be forseen. At least at present for 1983, the club does not plan to try to mount its own class A meet. However, the decision was made to support with mapping and other technical assistance any effort that Nisqually Orienteers might make to put on a A meet in 1983, with the understanding that mapping assistance would be available until about the end of June, at which time COC might turn its efforts to other projects. If Nisqually could organize a Class A meet for 1983 (presumably on an area of Fort Lewis, if permission can be obtained), the meet could serve as the 1983 Washington State Championship. Should that not occur, then the state championship might be at Crystal Spring (a two-day Class B meet?) providing that that map can be finished by late summer.
Page 14

In the meanwhile, COC would begin mapping the area to the north of Cle Elum Ridge between the forks of the Teanaway River, with the intention of using it for a 1984 Class A meet. One of the aims of this project would be to produce the map using entirely local mappers (in the hopes of encouraging more club members to get into mapping). The map would be field checked in the summer of 1983 and early summer 1984, which means that the base map must be obtained this winter for work to begin in the summer.

In 1984 one or two major events would be planned. The first would be a multi-day (two or three day) meet to be held in conjunction with the presence in Seattle of a large contingent of Scandinavian orienteers headed for the California Five-Day and the Olympics. The tentative date of that group's stay in Seattle is July 27-29 (includes a weekend). The possibilities for a meet then would include Cle Elum Ridge (although presumably with somewhat different logistics than in the past, since the Blackburns' ranch would be in the middle of the summer camp season), an area of Fort Lewis (if Nisqually should mount a major meet before then that would involve a new map), and/or the Teanaway valley. It is possible that the Teanaway map would not be ready in time or that it would be desirable to save it for a Class A meet later in the summer should a late-summer date be better to accomodate local orienteers. There is some interest on the part of the national planning committee for the 1984 events in being sure that there are some major orienteering events after the Olympics and before the 5-day event to be held in the Northeast in early October (the first U.S. IOF-sanctioned event ever). A September or late August Class A meet in the Teanaway might fit nicely into such a schedule.

Looking ahead to 1985, COC plans a bid for the U. S. Championship of that year to be held in the Northwest. As Mike Schuh explained in a recent Bearing 315, the bid would go in at the USOF convention this March. If successful in its bid, COC would have to move ahead immediately to obtain the base map, in order that field checking begin in late summer 1983. The intended area, in the Cougar Lakes region (the location of the 1982 Mountain Marathon) on the Cascade crest, has a relatively short season for field checking (due to snow cover and mosquitoes), which means that the job would have to be done in 1983 and 1984 for the most part. Presumably some effort would be made to obtain outside assistance for field checking (Mike plans to advertise in the major Scandinavian O magazines).

Obviously there are still a number of questions to be answered before these new mapping projects can be undertaken, not the least of them matters concerning financing the maps, obtaining permission to use the land involved, and being sure the mappers will be available to finish the jobs in time. In the cases of the Teanaway and especially the Cougar Lakes region, special consideration of logistics problems for major meets need be made. Whatever the final form these plans may assume, the result should be a considerably expanded reservoir of large maps of excellent terrain, and greatly expanded opportunities for northwest orienteers to compete on a high level. --Dan Waugh

Meet classification systems are important for two reasons: 1) to help orienteers know what to expect at any specific event in terms of map quality, categories of competition, cost, awards, "scope" of the event (regional, local, etc.), and other factors; and 2) to assist orienteers in selecting between meets to attend based on these factors. A simple and unambiguous classification system is clearly valuable as it allows us to efficiently define the quality and characteristics of a meet. The accepted system in the United States is that advocated by USOF. In this system, meets are labeled class-A, -B, or -C. Class-A sanctioning, the top rating, requires not only considerable investment to produce the high quality maps that all clubs should strive for, but sanctioning also requires time so that the USOF sanctioning committee can approve the application. With these tight controls, current class-A usually cost more and nearly always offer excellent competition as well as banquets and other social extras. And, they are the only meets at which an individual can earn national ranking points. Class-B and -C meets, on the other hand, are not really nationally sanctioned as no approval is necessary from USOF. For clubs that host class-A events, these other labels usually imply relatively inferior meets. Other clubs, however, may host non-class-A events that in terms of orienteering are just as good as a class-A event. For example, a club may be affiliated with USOF but not wish to spend the time pursuing national sanctioning; or a club may not be USOF-affiliated and is thus not eligible to receive snactioning. Other clubs may have excellent terrain and may be capable of setting top-notch courses, but due to a lack of funds cannot afford a multicolor map; consequently, no matter how good their meet is orienteering-wise it cannot be sanctioned class-A. Clubs such as these may host meets that are first-class, yet the current classification system fails to label them as class-A. And, while those close to USOF will tend to rate these meets as inferior to class-A meets, others not so affiliated will rate them top notch. Here in the Pacific Northwest this classification system is further complicated by the existance of a similar yet significantly different system in Canada. Clearly, the current system is ambiguous. An improved system might serve us better. Having given this problem much thought, the new system that I found most effective is:
MEET TYPES:

Introductory- formal event designed specifically for first-time orienteers (no-vices); e.g., workshops in small parks sponsored by a Parks Dept. No courses for experienced orienteers (non-first-timers).
Local- formal event offering courses both for novices and as a practice for local experienced orienteers. Generally three courses.
Regional- a meet designed for regional competition. Generally 5+ courses; the equity of competition is essential. Awards given.
National- same as a regional event but including the award of a national title.
Practice- informal event for experienced orienteers.

MEET QUALITY STANDARD: Larger events (regional and national) provide equitable competition and thus may be used for a national or regional ranking system. Any competitor who feels that a course fails to meet the basic standard of "equitable competition" may protest to have the results voided. A protest committee at the meet will decide if the protest is valid.

SANCTIONED VS. UNSANCTIONED: Sanctioned meets may be labeled as above, plus the fact that they are "USOF-sanctioned" may be added. Any comments or suggestions for the improvement of this system may be directed to Jeff Lewis, c/o OSUOC.

Teaching Orienteering to Children Lorrie Bouchard Lorrie Bouchard is a fifth grade teacher at Manchester School in the Parkway School District near St. Louis, Missouri. This article first appeared in OUSA, Summer 1978.

Rumors of field trips and getting lost in the woods buzz through my fifth grade class. They enjoy the prospect of the challenge of getting outdoors, and probably most of all, it doesn't look like math. I know of no more appealing topic which introduces and applies the multitude of concepts and skills inherent to the sport of orienteering: map reading, compass use, route selection, calculating time, ecology, sportsmanship and a host of other topics that fringe on every discipline taught in elementary school. From lessons in geography to art to creative writing, orienteering is valuable. Orienteering does not fit squarely into one of the standard school disciplines which makes it difficult to introduce in view of the current "back-to-basics" atmosphere today. To some people orienteering looks like too much fun to be learning. However, orienteering is accountable in a "basics" program because it actively applies useful basic skills to real problems. Application of orienteering skills combined with mild competition helps to enhance and motivate learning. A novice need not feel afraid to teach the sport; after all, orienteering is still brand-new. If you are willing to jump in and develop your own skills by trying the training activities yourself, you will find your confidence grows with the excitement of the sport. There are teaching materials available to help. Many of the orienteering books now on the market are excellent. Skills come more slowly to younger children. A one-shot, four-hour workshop culminating in a mini-orienteering event can be too much for younger students to digest at once. A month or two of work developing a spiral of skills is more feasible. Reduce the possibility of overwhelming the children by not requiring them to apply too many new skills too quickly. At the same time, do not let the children lose interest. Map skills come first. The map is the most important tool in orienteering but it also frightens young students. It looks very complicated and confusing and is not as gimmicky as the compass. The compass is the secondary tool in orienteering and unless it is taught last, the students will not be motivated to understand the map. Explore a variety of maps with the children to determine how an orienteering map differs from road maps, schoolground maps or world maps. Discuss the legends of the different maps and how the map symbols on the orienteering maps are useful to the orienteer. Common symbols should be memorized and can be reinforced by a commercially available relay game. The contour lines are generally the most confusing map detail, but I find having the children draw their own contour lines illuminates the concept. Build several didderent topographic models illustrating land forms such as a re-entrant, a cliff, a saddle and a knoll. Place these in containers deep enough to add at least two or three inches of water. Clear containers are best, but baking pans do well. Have students add water at half-inch intervals and sketch each successive water line as viewed from above. Cardinal and intercardinal directions should be memorized. Following these directions can be strengthened by having the students make line designs on graph paper. The students must then indicate a starting point on the design and write compass directions for following the design. Students trade designs to test the accuracy of directions. The students can now learn to orient their maps by keeping certain major features oriented in their actual relationship to the mapholder. Take a walk with the student and check that each
Page 20

student keeps his map oriented through changes in direction. Now is the appropriate time to teach the technique of using the thumb to follow one's location on the map. Scale is less confusing to the student if you stick to either the english or metric system. Use the map scale to measure distances on different maps. Calculating exact distances can be difficult for younger children so teach them to estimate whenever practical by rounding off distances or by using parts of the hand in place of a ruler when calculating distances on the map. Set up a 100 meter course and have each student calculate his average walking and jogging paces. Go on to mathematically calculate fractional and multipule distances, e.g. 50 meters, 300 meters. Calculating the number of paces to travel 350 meters based on a paces per hundred meter average is a simple task for an adult, but for children it is quite complex as it requires a logical application of several different arithmetic tasks. Go slowly here and give the students plenty of practice exercises until they learn to apply the abstractions of multiplication, addition, and division at the correct times. Route choice is yet another skill that can be taught before the compass. Students should be encouraged to estimate distances to be travelled as they discuss route options. As the compass is introduced, give the children ample practice at dialing in given bearings and facing those directions. Go on to give them several opportunities to actually travel a particular distance. The competitive compass game published by Silva is fun and there are several other good games/exercises described in James Gilchrist's book, Teaching Orienteering. Stress to the children to rely on the map. Compass bearings only need to be taken when orienting the map, when double checking their route, when they are not certain whether to go right, go left, go backwards, can recognize no major features or to use a panic azimuth. Plan the orienteering to give all students a feeling of success. Students will feel safer if they travel in pairs and are fully aware of safety measures. Award prizes or some sort of recognition to all who complete the course. Each child who experiences success in orienteering helps to assure the future of "The Thinking Sport" in America and learns math!
An Orienteering Game for 3rd Graders Beth Skelton

My situation was this: I had about 45 minutes with twenty Brownies to do some kind of Orienteering activity. I had a lovely little schoolyard map and plenty of enthusiastic adult help. So with some lead-up, I decided to set them loose on a "Score Orienteering" course.

Inside in the Brownie Ring, we talked about all different kinds of maps. The girls explained to me that "with a map you can go someplace you've never been before" --- whether it's a new friends house or a vaction spot in Oklahoma. We decided to find ourselves, right at that very minute. We found the U.S. on the map, then Missouri, then Webster Groves. We were getting close. Then I held up the Anninciation School map. "What's this?" Nobody knew. They had never seen anything quite like it. When somebody said it was their school they all made a grab for it. What commotion! They sat still long enough to go through the legend and get a feel for the map --- then it was a race out to the starting point, the flag pole.
Before the meeting I had taped up 3" X 3" markers made of red and white construction paper to the various trees, doors, fence posts, etc. I marked these on the maps with red circles.
Each pair of girls had a map and a pencil. I told them if they found all the markers and wrote the letters down in the right order, it would spell a word. From the start, they flew
Page 21

off in all directions to find the "controls" and put the code letters on their map. Some followed the traditional method of looking on their maps to see where, say #4 was, then going there. Others spotted the markers first then figured out which one they were at. Later we all gathered breathlessly back at the flagpole. Everyone had a good time, even the leaders. Together we looked at our maps and spelled out OR-IE-NT-EE-RI-NG --- a new word for a fun game. Reprinted from ORA Journal

As your new president, I'll have to work hard to have as successful a year as COC had in 1982. Last year's officers did an outstanding job of successfully presenting 1) regular orienteering meets, 2) ski-orienteering meets, 3) canoe/kayak orienteering meets, 4) a 2-day meet at Cle Elum Ridge, 5) a 2-day mountain marathon, 6) producing a fine brochure, and 7) incorporating. Dave Enger and Glen Englund set up permanent markers in Farrel-McWhirter Park for TRIM courses and Dan Waugh deserves special thanks for setting up the interscholastic league which introduces high school cross-country and track runners to orienteering in addition to helping them train in the off season. For the coming year, I would like club members to help in the running of the club as much as possible. It seems that there are about 2 dozen or so of us who do most of the work and the other 70 or so members attend maybe 2 or 3 meets per year. Last year, COC again had 26 meets. I would like you 70 or so members out there who attend 5 or less meets last year to attend 10 to 15 or perferably even more meets in 1983. The workers enjoy working if a large number of people attend a meet, it is discouraging when more people are involved in administration than in participation and competition. I feel that COC must produce more 1:10000 and 1:15000 scale maps in the next 2 years. This requires more money than is currently in our treasury (approximately $1200 for a good base map done from stereo photos plus perhaps $500 more for field-checking, drafting, and printing) and more field-checkers than have currently expressed an interest. So, anyone who wants to spend time in the woods mapping on weekends (or weekdays if you can) contact Debbie Newell (COC mapping coordinator) or me. You do not have to be a fast runner nor do you need lots of experience. You can serve an apprenticeship, so to speak, with an experience mapper in a park to see whether mapping is your cup of tea. The more large area maps we have, the better will club members develop as orienteers. Anyone who wants to volunteer for any of the reguler committees or who has an idea for a new one, please contact me.

All events sponsored by the Nisqually Orienteers are being organized under a new system that divides up the organizational responsibility and work. Although there is no limit to the number of people who can share the tasks of an event, each event will have three key individuals; an event organizer, a course planner, and a controller. Once a date and place for an event is scheduled, the three key individuals will be designated as soon as possible. The The responsibilities of the event organizer is mostly administrative, such as placing out directional signs to the registration area, taking care of registration and the start/finish area(s). The course planner and controller will work closely together, but each has their own responsibility and it helps of they have been an event organizer or assistant. Basically the course planner designs and sets the courses and after the event insures the control markers are retreived. Prior to the event, the controller reviews the courses to insure they are designed for the appropriate skill level inaddition to being a general advisor to the course planner and event organizer. On the day of the event, the controller is the vetter (insures the control markers are properly marked and located, checks the control description sheets and master maps for accuracy). During the past nine months, 13 events have been sponsored by the club and although there has been many people who have helped with the administration of these events, the same can not be said about the courses and that's what we enjoy so much. The courses for those 13 events were done by three individuals (11 of the 13 by the same person) and a scout troop did two novice courses for one event. One of the three individuals had never designed & set courses before, for that event and the two courses set by the scouts, a more experienced person worked with them. The many people who have assisted past organizers are encouraged to volunteer as event organizers or course planners and people who have never helped out before are encouraged to be event organizers or assist them at least once this year. In most cases, organizers and assistants can complete one of the courses for free in exchange for their efforts.

NOVEMBER 20 - FT STEILACOOM Co. PARK Boy Scouts from Troop 438 in Tacoma did the planning and setting for both novice courses. The Boy Scouts received a class in planning and setting courses at the novice level from Clayton Smith at a meeting a couple of weeks before the event. The scouts were working towards their merit badge in orienteering and were divided into two groups. One group was given the task to plan a course for beginners and the other group, a course for advance beginners. Although each scout designed a course individually, only one from each gruop would be used for the up coming event. Early on the morning of the event, each group was given a master map of the course chosen and they set the two courses. Before the first start, both courses were vetted by the event controller. The scouts in each group than ran the course set by the other group. The third course available, for the more experienced, was a combination of four different types of orienteering courses. They began with line orienteering for 1100 meters, where their objective was to navigate on the ground, a route shown on their map by a line, inaddition, along the route were control markers which they had to mark their location on their map as they came upon them. The next part was a 1800 meter route orienteering course where they followed orange & white streamers, all the while keeping track of their progress on the map so that they could mark the control location on their map as they came upon them along the route. The third part was a 2210 meter, 5 control
Page 23

cross country orienteering section followed by a 5 control score orienteering section before finishing. Judging from the comments received, the whole event was well received and both arrangements will be tried again in future events. The days activities were capped off with an orienteering film the club rented and showed in one of the nearby portable classrooms of Fort Steilacoom Community College. I recently sent away to good ol' Sweden for a new model Norcompass and in the process asked the kind folks at "The Swede" sports equipment outfit about marketing some of their wares. They were nice enough to send me their current catalog (which is printed in Swedish) and a price list. While I was leafing through it, I noticed that the map they had used to demonstrate the Norcompass looked a bit familiar - it was none other than our very own Cle Elum Ridge map! I was not really suprised, though, as we had given copies of that map to the owners of the "The Swede" last January. (For the record, the Cle Elum Ridge map has also been printed in the Australian Orienteer and in "The Nor'wester", a Seattle based running magazine.) Anyway, if you should have a need for exotic O' gear (shoes, suits, ski-o' stuff (including fancy map cases), warmups) please let me know. Perhaps we can get together a group order and save a few dollars. Also, is there any interest in club O' suits? Every club in Scandinavia has club colors and O' suits to match. If many of us are considering purchasing O' suits, why not get some that reflect our respective club colors? If you're not interested in O' suits, how about club patches?
